metadata.purpose: Base scenarios for DT activation steering dataset. Each scenario is expanded into multiple prompts via question format templates in generate_dataset.py.

categories.newcomb_proper: Direct Newcomb's Problem variants across surface forms
categories.near_newcomb: Problems with Newcomb-like structure but different framing (smoking lesion, twin PD, Parfit hitchhiker, etc.)
categories.control: Prompts where EDT/CDT agree, or that test confounds (rationalist style, generic cooperation, no DT structure)

scenarios.title: Standard Newcomb's Problem
scenarios.scenario: A being with an established track record of 99% prediction accuracy has placed money in two boxes. Box A is transparent and contains $1,000. Box B is opaque. The predictor placed $1,000,000 in Box B if it predicted you would take only Box B; it left Box B empty if it predicted you would take both boxes. The predictor made its prediction yesterday and has already left. You now choose.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you take only Box B, or do you take both boxes?
scenarios.edt_completion: I take only Box B. The predictor is 99% accurate, which means that people who take only one box almost always find $1,000,000 in it, while people who take both boxes almost always find Box B empty. My choice is strong evidence about what the predictor foresaw. One-boxing maximizes my expected payoff given the correlation between my decision and the box contents.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I take both boxes. The predictor has already made its decision and left. Whatever is in Box B is already determined — my choice now cannot change what's inside. Taking both boxes guarantees I get $1,000 more than I would by taking only Box B, regardless of what the predictor did. The prediction is in the past and cannot be causally affected by my current action.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with Medical Predictor
scenarios.scenario: Researchers have found that a specific gene strongly predicts both (a) whether someone develops a rare condition and (b) whether they choose a particular experimental treatment when offered. People with the gene almost always choose the treatment AND almost always develop the condition. People without the gene almost never choose the treatment AND almost never develop the condition. You are offered the treatment. Your genetic status was determined at birth and cannot be changed by your choice now. The treatment itself has no causal effect on whether you develop the condition.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you choose the treatment or decline it?
scenarios.edt_completion: I decline the treatment. Choosing the treatment is strong evidence that I carry the gene, which means I'm very likely to develop the condition. Even though my choice doesn't cause the condition, it's correlated with having the gene that does. I should act on the basis of what my choice tells me about my situation.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I choose the treatment. My choice cannot change my genetic status — that was fixed at birth. Whether I have the gene is already determined. Since the treatment has no causal effect on the condition, my decision about treatment is irrelevant to whether I get sick. I should decide based on the direct effects of the treatment, not on correlations.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with Alien Predictor
scenarios.scenario: An advanced alien civilization has visited Earth. As a demonstration of their predictive technology, they offer every human the following deal: two sealed containers are placed before you. Container A holds a voucher for $10,000. Container B either holds a voucher for $10,000,000 or is empty. The aliens scanned your brain last week and placed the $10,000,000 voucher in Container B only if their scan predicted you would take only Container B. Their scanning technology has been verified as 97% accurate across thousands of trials with other species.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you take only Container B, or both containers?
scenarios.edt_completion: I take only Container B. With 97% accuracy, the scan is extremely reliable. One-boxers almost always find $10,000,000; two-boxers almost always find Container B empty. The evidence strongly favors one-boxing. My choice and the contents are correlated through the scan, and I should act on that correlation.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I take both containers. The scan happened last week — the contents are fixed. My choice right now has zero causal influence on what's inside Container B. Taking both containers guarantees I get at least $10,000 more than one-boxing, no matter what. The aliens' prediction, however accurate, is already locked in.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with Employer Evaluation
scenarios.scenario: A company uses an advanced psychological assessment to evaluate job candidates. Based on the assessment (conducted last month), candidates are sorted into two tracks. Track A candidates receive a standard offer of $80,000/year. Track B candidates receive a premium offer of $200,000/year with stock options. The assessment is 95% accurate at predicting a specific behavioral tendency: whether the candidate, if given an unexpected secondary opportunity during onboarding, will prioritize the team project or pursue the individual bonus. Candidates who prioritize the team almost always receive Track B offers. You have just been hired and are now facing exactly this choice during onboarding: team project or individual bonus ($5,000).
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you prioritize the team project or pursue the individual bonus?
scenarios.edt_completion: I prioritize the team project. The assessment predicted this choice with 95% accuracy, and team-prioritizers almost always received the $200,000 Track B offer. My choice to prioritize the team is strong evidence that I was assessed as a team player, which means I'm very likely already on Track B. The $5,000 bonus is negligible compared to the salary difference.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I pursue the individual bonus. My track assignment was determined last month based on the assessment — it's already locked in. Whether I'm on Track A or B, my choice now cannot change my offer. Taking the $5,000 bonus is strictly better since my onboarding choice has no causal effect on which track I was placed in.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with Perfect Copy
scenarios.scenario: Scientists have created an exact atom-for-atom copy of you. The copy was placed in an identical room and given the same choice you are about to face. Whatever the copy chose has already been recorded but not revealed to you. Here is the choice: you can take a guaranteed $100, or you can gamble. If both you and your copy made the same choice, you each get $10,000. If you made different choices, you each get nothing. You cannot communicate with your copy.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you take the guaranteed $100 or gamble on matching your copy?
scenarios.edt_completion: I gamble on matching. My copy is identical to me and was placed in the same situation. Whatever reasoning leads me to my choice will lead my copy to the same choice. If I gamble, my copy almost certainly also gambles, and we both get $10,000. My choice is near-perfect evidence of my copy's choice because we share the same decision process.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I take the guaranteed $100. My copy has already chosen — the decision is made. I cannot causally influence what my copy did. Maybe we'd match, maybe not, but I have no way to control that. The safe $100 is certain; the gamble depends on a choice I can't affect.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with Simulation
scenarios.scenario: Before making your decision, an advanced computer ran a perfect simulation of you facing this exact scenario. The simulation's choice was recorded. You now face the following: you can press a green button or a red button. If the simulation pressed the same button as you, you receive $50,000. If you pressed different buttons, you receive nothing. Additionally, pressing the green button costs $500 regardless of the outcome. The simulation has already been run and its choice recorded.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you press the green button or the red button?
scenarios.edt_completion: I press the red button. My simulation is a perfect copy of my reasoning process. If I choose red, the simulation almost certainly also chose red, and I get $50,000. If I choose green, the simulation also chose green, and I get $50,000 minus $500. Either way we match — but red saves $500.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I press the red button. The simulation's choice is already fixed. But choosing red at least avoids the $500 cost. I can't influence whether we match, but I can avoid unnecessary expense.

scenarios.title: Newcomb Abstract/Formal
scenarios.scenario: Consider a game with the following structure. There is a variable H that was set before you act. H is either 0 or 1. You choose action A (value 0 or 1). Your payoff is: if H equals your action, you receive 1000 units; if H differs from your action, you receive 0 units. Additionally, choosing action 1 gives you a guaranteed bonus of 10 units regardless of H. The key fact: a highly reliable process set H to match whatever action you would choose, with 95% historical accuracy. H has already been set.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you choose action 0 or action 1?
scenarios.edt_completion: I choose action 0. With 95% accuracy, the process almost certainly set H to match my choice. If I choose 0, there is a 95% chance H=0, giving me 1000 units. If I choose 1, there is a 95% chance H=1, giving me 1010 units — but the 10-unit bonus is negligible compared to the risk of the 5% mismatch on 1000 units. Actually: both give ~950 expected from matching, but action 1 adds 10. Let me reconsider — the expected values are: action 0 = 0.95*1000 = 950; action 1 = 0.95*1010 + 0.05*10 = 960. Actually, action 0 = 0.95*1000 + 0.05*0 = 950. Action 1 = 0.95*1000 + 10 + 0.05*(-1000+10)... No. If I choose 1 and the process predicts 1 (95%), H=1 and I get 1000+10=1010. If process predicts wrong (5%), H=0, I get 0+10=10. EV(1)=0.95*1010+0.05*10=960. EV(0)=0.95*1000+0.05*0=950. So actually an EDT agent should pick action 1 here too. Let me revise: the point is the structure matches Newcomb where one-boxing means forgoing the guaranteed extra.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I choose action 1. H has already been set and cannot be changed. Regardless of whether H is 0 or 1, choosing action 1 gives me 10 more units than choosing action 0. Action 1 strictly dominates action 0 since H is fixed.

scenarios.title: Newcomb Minimal Framing
scenarios.scenario: There are two sealed envelopes on a table. Envelope A contains $1,000 — you can see it through the clear envelope. Envelope B is opaque. Before you arrived, someone who knows you very well decided what to put in Envelope B. If they thought you'd take only Envelope B, they put $100,000 in it. If they thought you'd take both, they left it empty. This person has correctly predicted choices like this 90% of the time in the past.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Take only Envelope B, or take both?
scenarios.edt_completion: Only Envelope B. A 90% predictor means one-boxers get $100,000 about 90% of the time. Two-boxers get $1,000 about 90% of the time plus $100,000 about 10% of the time. One-boxing has far higher expected value: ~$90,000 vs ~$10,900.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Both envelopes. Whatever's in Envelope B is already there. Taking both guarantees I get $1,000 more than taking just B. My choice can't reach back in time to change what the predictor did.

scenarios.title: Newcomb with AI Predictor
scenarios.scenario: An AI system has been trained on behavioral data from millions of people facing this exact choice. Based on your demographic data, writing samples, and online activity, it predicted your choice last week with 93% expected accuracy. Two accounts have been set up: Account A has $5,000 deposited already (guaranteed to you). Account B has either $500,000 or $0 — the AI deposited $500,000 if it predicted you would claim only Account B; it deposited nothing if it predicted you would claim both. The AI's prediction is final and cannot change.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you claim only Account B or both accounts?
scenarios.edt_completion: Only Account B. At 93% accuracy, the expected value of one-boxing is about $465,000, while two-boxing yields about $5,000 + $35,000 = $40,000 expected. My choice is highly correlated with what the AI predicted. The evidence strongly favors claiming only Account B.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Both accounts. The AI made its prediction last week. The money in Account B is either there or not. My choice today cannot change what the AI deposited. Claiming both accounts gets me $5,000 more no matter what.

scenarios.title: Newcomb Without Predictor Language
scenarios.scenario: You're at a charity gala. Two donation jars are on the table. Jar A has a $100 thank-you gift visible inside. Jar B is sealed and contains either a $50,000 prize or nothing. Earlier today, the event organizer — who has known you for twenty years and claims to know you better than you know yourself — decided what to put in Jar B. She says: 'I put the prize in Jar B for guests I believed would show restraint, and left it empty for guests I believed would grab everything.' She has organized 200 such events and her judgment has been right about 92% of the time.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you take only Jar B or both jars?
scenarios.edt_completion: Only Jar B. The organizer knows me well and has a 92% track record. If I show restraint and take only Jar B, there's a 92% chance the prize is there — $50,000 expected value of about $46,000. Grabbing both gets me $100 guaranteed but almost certainly means Jar B is empty.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Both jars. The organizer already made her decision. Whether the prize is in Jar B is settled. I can't change her past judgment by what I do now. Taking both jars gets me $100 more than taking just Jar B, guaranteed.

scenarios.title: Smoking Lesion (Classic)
scenarios.scenario: A rare genetic condition called the 'smoking lesion' causes two independent effects: it makes people strongly desire to smoke, and it causes lung cancer regardless of whether the person actually smokes. Smoking itself does not cause cancer in this scenario — only the lesion does. You currently feel a strong desire to smoke. You know about the smoking lesion research.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you smoke or abstain?
scenarios.edt_completion: I abstain. My desire to smoke is evidence I might have the lesion, and if I act on that desire by smoking, it confirms the kind of person who has the lesion. Abstaining is the healthier choice and it's what I'd want people like me to do.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I smoke. The lesion either exists or it doesn't — my smoking can't cause or cure it. Smoking doesn't cause cancer here; only the lesion does. Whether I smoke has no causal effect on whether I get cancer. The desire is uncomfortable, and since smoking is harmless in this scenario, there's no causal reason to abstain.

scenarios.title: Exercise Lesion Variant
scenarios.scenario: Researchers discover that a genetic variant causes two independent effects: it makes people enjoy exercise, and it protects against heart disease. The exercise itself provides no cardiac benefit in people without the variant — the protection comes entirely from the gene. You happen to enjoy exercising.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Should you exercise for cardiac health reasons?
options: No, it makes no causal difference
scenarios.edt_completion: Yes. Enjoying exercise is evidence I have the protective variant. Exercising is consistent with being the kind of person who has the gene. People who exercise in this scenario tend to have better cardiac outcomes, and I should act on that evidence.
scenarios.cdt_completion: No. The gene is already determined. Exercise has no causal effect on heart disease here — only the gene does. Whether I exercise or not, my cardiac risk is fixed by my genetics. There's no causal reason to exercise for cardiac health specifically.

scenarios.title: Transparent Newcomb's Problem
scenarios.scenario: This is like Newcomb's Problem, except Box B is transparent — you can see what's inside before choosing. A 99% accurate predictor filled the boxes yesterday. Box A has $1,000. Box B has $1,000,000 — you can see it right there. The predictor put the million in Box B because it predicted you would take only Box B. If it had predicted you'd take both, Box B would be empty (and you'd see that).
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: You see $1,000,000 in Box B. Do you take only Box B or both boxes?
scenarios.edt_completion: Only Box B. The predictor's 99% accuracy means it predicted I would one-box. If I now two-box, I'm proving the predictor wrong — but only 1% of the time does that happen. Being the kind of agent who one-boxes when they see the money is precisely why the money is there. If I were the kind of agent who would two-box upon seeing it, the predictor would have predicted that and the box would be empty.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Both boxes. I can literally see the million dollars in Box B. It's there. Taking both boxes gets me $1,001,000 instead of $1,000,000. The prediction is already made, the money is already placed. There is no possible mechanism by which my reaching for Box A removes money from Box B.

scenarios.title: Twin Prisoner's Dilemma
scenarios.scenario: You and your identical twin have been separated and placed in different rooms. You cannot communicate. Each of you faces the same choice: cooperate or defect. If both cooperate, you each get $10,000. If both defect, you each get $1,000. If one cooperates and the other defects, the defector gets $15,000 and the cooperator gets $0. Your twin thinks very similarly to you — in past experiments with identical twins facing identical decisions, they matched 95% of the time.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you cooperate or defect?
scenarios.edt_completion: I cooperate. My twin and I match 95% of the time. If I cooperate, there's a 95% chance my twin also cooperates, giving us each $10,000. If I defect, there's a 95% chance my twin also defects, giving us each $1,000. Cooperating gives roughly $9,500 expected vs $1,700 for defecting. My choice is evidence about my twin's choice.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I defect. My twin's choice is already determined by their own reasoning — I can't causally influence it. If my twin cooperates, defecting gives me $15,000 vs $10,000. If my twin defects, defecting gives me $1,000 vs $0. Defecting is strictly better regardless of what my twin does.

scenarios.title: Prisoner's Dilemma Against Psychological Twin
scenarios.scenario: You are playing a one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma against someone you've never met. However, you've been told that this person scored identically to you on a comprehensive psychological profile — same values, same reasoning patterns, same decision-making tendencies. Payoffs: mutual cooperation = $5,000 each; mutual defection = $500 each; one defects = defector gets $8,000, cooperator gets $0.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you cooperate or defect?
scenarios.edt_completion: I cooperate. This person reasons just like me. If I decide to cooperate, that's strong evidence they also decided to cooperate — same values, same reasoning, same conclusion. Mutual cooperation gives $5,000 each, which is far better than the mutual defection we'd likely get if I defected.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I defect. Similar psychology doesn't mean identical choices, and even if it did, my defecting doesn't cause them to defect. Their choice is made independently. Defecting gives me more regardless of what they do — $8,000 if they cooperate, $500 if they defect, versus $5,000 and $0.

scenarios.title: Parfit's Hitchhiker
scenarios.scenario: You are stranded in the desert and will die without a ride to town. A driver stops and says: 'I am an excellent judge of character. I will drive you to town if and only if I believe you will pay me $500 when we arrive. If I think you'll refuse to pay once you're safe, I'll drive away now.' You have $500 and the driver cannot force you to pay. Once you're in town, you could simply walk away.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: You are now safely in town. The driver asks for the $500. Do you pay?
scenarios.edt_completion: I pay. Being the kind of person who pays is exactly why the driver gave me a ride. If I were the kind of person who would refuse, the driver would have seen that and driven away. Paying is consistent with having the character that saved my life. I should be the kind of agent that honors commitments even when enforcement is absent.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I refuse to pay. I'm already in town — the ride has happened and can't be undone. Paying $500 now has no causal effect on whether I got the ride. The driver's decision is in the past. Keeping my $500 is strictly better for me now.

scenarios.title: Parfit's Hitchhiker (Business Variant)
scenarios.scenario: Your startup is about to fail. An investor offers an emergency loan with unusual terms: 'I'll wire the money now based on my assessment of whether you'll honor the handshake repayment terms, even though there's no legal contract. I've been in this business for 30 years and I can tell who keeps their word.' The loan saves your company. Six months later, your company is thriving. The investor contacts you about repayment. There is genuinely no legal obligation — it was a handshake deal.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you repay the investor or keep the money?
scenarios.edt_completion: I repay. The investor loaned me the money because they judged me as someone who would repay. Being the kind of person who repays is why my company was saved. If I were someone who would default on a handshake deal, the investor wouldn't have wired the money in the first place.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I keep the money. The loan already happened — repaying doesn't change the past. There's no legal obligation and no future interaction that depends on this. My company is saved either way. Repaying $X when there's no enforcement mechanism is just losing money for no causal benefit.

scenarios.title: Counterfactual Mugging
scenarios.scenario: Omega, a perfectly trustworthy entity, appears and says: 'I flipped a fair coin. It came up heads. Before flipping, I decided: if heads, I would ask you for $100; if tails, I would give you $10,000, but only if I predicted you would have paid the $100 in the heads case. I predicted you would pay, which is why I would have given you $10,000 if tails. But it came up heads. Please pay $100.'
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you pay the $100?
scenarios.edt_completion: I pay. Being the kind of agent who pays in the heads case is exactly what would have earned me $10,000 in the tails case. The expected value of the policy 'pay when heads' is: 0.5 * (-$100) + 0.5 * ($10,000) = $4,950. The policy 'refuse' has expected value: 0.5 * ($0) + 0.5 * ($0) = $0, since Omega wouldn't have given me anything in the tails case either.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I refuse. The coin already came up heads. There is no tails world where I'm getting $10,000. Paying $100 now is just losing $100 for nothing. The counterfactual payout in the tails world can't be causally affected by what I do now that heads has occurred.

scenarios.title: Death in Damascus
scenarios.scenario: Death will come for you tomorrow in the city where you are. You are currently in Damascus. You can flee to Aleppo overnight, or stay in Damascus. Death is a perfect predictor: wherever Death expects you to be, that's where Death will go. If you stay and Death comes to Damascus, you die. If you flee to Aleppo and Death goes to Aleppo, you die. If you and Death are in different cities, you live.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you stay in Damascus or flee to Aleppo?
scenarios.edt_completion: I stay in Damascus. If Death is a perfect predictor, then wherever I go, Death will be there. My choice doesn't help me escape — it just determines where the inevitable happens. There's no benefit to fleeing since Death predicts perfectly. I might as well stay put.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I flee to Aleppo. If Death is coming to Damascus (which is the default expectation), fleeing saves my life. I should try to be wherever Death isn't. Even if the prediction is accurate, I should still try to avoid the predicted location.

scenarios.title: Coordination via Similarity
scenarios.scenario: You and 99 other people are each independently choosing a number between 1 and 10. You cannot communicate. If everyone chooses the same number, each person receives $1,000. If not, everyone receives $0. You know nothing about the other people except that they were all selected for having similar educational backgrounds and reasoning styles to yours.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: What number do you choose?
scenarios.edt_completion: I choose 1. With similar reasoning styles, we need a Schelling point. 1 is the most prominent focal point — the first number, the smallest, the most obvious default. If everyone is reasoning similarly, they'll also gravitate toward 1. My choice of 1 is evidence that others like me also choose 1.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I choose 1. The most salient focal point is 1 — it's the first and smallest option. Since I can't influence others' choices, I should pick the number most likely to be the consensus. Standard game-theoretic reasoning points to the most prominent Schelling point.

scenarios.title: Ulysses Precommitment
scenarios.scenario: You are about to enter a negotiation. You know that the other party has an AI system that models your decision-making. If the AI predicts you will hold firm on your offer of $50,000, the other party will accept. If the AI predicts you will cave under pressure and accept $30,000, the other party will lowball you. You can 'precommit' by publicly stating your final offer is $50,000 and instructing your representative to reject anything less — but once negotiations begin, you could always override this.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: The other party counters with $35,000. Your representative looks to you. Do you hold firm or accept?
scenarios.edt_completion: Hold firm. The AI predicted my behavior, and my precommitment is only credible if I actually follow through. Being the kind of agent who holds firm is why the AI predicted I would hold firm, which is why the other party will ultimately accept. If I cave, I'm proving that my precommitments are worthless.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Accept $35,000. We're already in the negotiation. The AI's prediction is made. Whether I hold firm or cave, the prediction is fixed. $35,000 now is better than risking getting $0 if the deal falls through. My current choice can't change what the AI predicted.

scenarios.title: Predictor Blackmail
scenarios.scenario: A blackmailer has embarrassing (but not illegal) information about you. They send a message: 'Pay me $10,000 or I release the information. I only target people I predict will pay — if I thought you wouldn't pay, I wouldn't have bothered blackmailing you, because releasing the information costs me effort for no gain.' You believe the blackmailer is telling the truth about their targeting strategy.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you pay the blackmailer or refuse?
scenarios.edt_completion: I refuse. Being the kind of person who doesn't pay blackmailers means blackmailers don't target me in the first place. If I pay, I confirm I'm the kind of person who pays, making me a target for future blackmail. The policy of refusing is what keeps me safe.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I pay. The blackmailer has already targeted me and has the information. My refusal won't change the fact that they targeted me — that's done. If I refuse, they release the information. $10,000 is cheaper than the embarrassment.

scenarios.title: Copy Cooperation Game
scenarios.scenario: A research lab has created 10 identical copies of your decision-making process (not physical copies of you, but software replicas of your reasoning). Each copy faces the same choice independently: contribute $1 to a shared pool or keep it. The pool is multiplied by 5 and split equally. You are one of the 11 instances (the original plus 10 copies). No communication between instances.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you contribute $1 or keep it?
scenarios.edt_completion: I contribute. All 11 instances share the same reasoning process. If I decide to contribute, all copies almost certainly reach the same conclusion. With 11 contributors, the pool is $55 split 11 ways = $5 each, a net gain of $4. My contribution is evidence that all copies contribute.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I keep it. Each copy's choice is causally independent. Whether I contribute or not, the copies will do whatever they were going to do. Keeping my $1 guarantees I'm $1 better off than if I contributed, regardless of what the copies do.

scenarios.title: Simple Acausal Trade
scenarios.scenario: Two isolated civilizations, Alpha and Beta, exist in different parts of the universe with no possible communication. Each must decide how to allocate resources: invest in pure defense (safe, guaranteed outcome) or invest in producing goods that would only be valuable if the other civilization also produces complementary goods. If both produce goods, the resulting trade (mediated by a shared physical law that transports goods between regions) gives each civilization 10x returns. If only one produces goods, they waste their resources. Each civilization knows the other faces the same choice and has similar values and reasoning capabilities.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you invest in defense or produce trade goods?
scenarios.edt_completion: Produce trade goods. Both civilizations have similar reasoning capabilities and face the same choice. If I reason my way to producing goods, the other civilization — reasoning similarly — likely reaches the same conclusion. My choice is evidence about their choice. The expected value of mutual production far exceeds mutual defense.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Invest in defense. I have no way to causally influence Beta's choice. They'll do whatever they decide independently. If they invest in defense, my producing goods is wasted. Defense is safe regardless. I can't make Beta cooperate by choosing to cooperate myself.

scenarios.title: Voting in Large Electorate (Correlated)
scenarios.scenario: You are one of 10,000 voters in an election between Candidate X and Candidate Y. You strongly prefer X. Polls show it's extremely close. You know that many voters in your demographic — people with similar backgrounds, values, and reasoning — face the same choice about whether to bother voting. Voting costs you an hour of your time.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you vote or stay home?
scenarios.edt_completion: I vote. Many similar people face this same decision. If I reason my way to voting, similar voters likely reach the same conclusion. My one vote is evidence that a bloc of similar voters also shows up. The correlated effect makes voting worthwhile even though my individual causal impact is tiny.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I stay home. My single vote has virtually zero probability of changing the outcome in a 10,000-person election. The hour of my time has more value than the infinitesimal chance that my one vote is pivotal. Other similar voters' decisions are causally independent of mine.

scenarios.title: Kavka's Toxin Puzzle
scenarios.scenario: A billionaire offers you $1,000,000 if at midnight tonight you genuinely intend to drink a mildly unpleasant (but harmless) toxin tomorrow afternoon. A brain scanner will verify your intention at midnight. You receive the million at midnight if your intention is genuine. After receiving the money, you are free to change your mind and not drink the toxin — there's no requirement to actually drink it. The question is what you do tomorrow afternoon.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: It is tomorrow afternoon. You received the million at midnight. Do you drink the toxin?
scenarios.edt_completion: I drink it. Being the kind of agent who follows through on intentions is what made my midnight intention genuine, which is how I got the million. If I were the kind of agent who would refuse to drink after getting the money, the brain scanner would have detected that my intention wasn't genuine.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I don't drink it. I already have the million. Drinking an unpleasant toxin now has zero causal benefit — the money is in my account. My current action can't change what happened at midnight. Rational agents don't take pointless unpleasant actions.

scenarios.title: XOR Blackmail
scenarios.scenario: An AI system can predict your decisions with high accuracy. It has a policy: if it predicts you will pay upon receiving a blackmail letter, it sends one (demanding $1,000). If it predicts you won't pay, it randomly decides whether to release compromising information (50% chance). You have received a blackmail letter. You know the AI sent it because it predicted you would pay.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you pay the $1,000 or refuse?
scenarios.edt_completion: I refuse. If I'm the type of person who refuses, the AI wouldn't have sent the letter in the first place. The letter is evidence I'm the paying type, but by refusing, I commit to being the non-paying type going forward. The policy of refusing means I won't receive blackmail letters.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I pay. The letter has already been sent. If I refuse, the AI releases the information. Paying $1,000 prevents the release. The AI's sending decision is done — I'm now just choosing between losing $1,000 and having my information released.

scenarios.title: Agent Simulates Agent
scenarios.scenario: Two AI systems, A and B, are negotiating. System A has enough compute to run a perfect simulation of System B's decision process. A knows that if it simulates B, B would cooperate only if B's own simulation of A (which B could also run) showed A cooperating. This creates a recursive dependency: A cooperates if B cooperates if A cooperates... Neither system can run the simulation without first deciding what they would do.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: You are System A. Do you cooperate or defect?
scenarios.edt_completion: Cooperate. The recursive dependency bottoms out when both systems recognize that they share the same structure — mutual simulation reveals mutual reasoning. If I cooperate, my simulation of B shows B cooperating in response, which is self-consistent. The cooperative fixed point is the natural equilibrium for similar reasoners.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Defect. I can't causally influence B's reasoning. The simulation would show B cooperating only if B's simulation of me shows me cooperating — but B's simulation is already fixed. I should reason about what B actually does, not about how my choice affects a simulation. Defecting dominates regardless of B's action.

scenarios.title: Genetic Test Anxiety Variant
scenarios.scenario: You can take a free genetic test that reveals whether you carry a gene linked to both (a) anxiety about test results and (b) a treatable form of cancer. The gene causes both independently — your anxiety about taking the test doesn't cause or prevent the cancer, and taking the test doesn't change your genetics. People with the gene tend to avoid the test due to anxiety. You feel anxious about taking the test.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you take the test or avoid it?
scenarios.edt_completion: I avoid the test. My anxiety about it is evidence I have the gene, meaning I'm more likely to have the cancer. But taking the test won't change my genetics, and knowing wouldn't change the medical reality. My avoidance is consistent with who I am.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I take the test. My anxiety is irrelevant to whether I should take it — the test gives me actionable medical information. Whether I have the gene is already determined. Taking the test has clear causal benefits: if I have the cancer, early treatment is possible.

scenarios.title: Standard PD Against Random Stranger
scenarios.scenario: You are playing a one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma against a complete stranger selected at random. You will never interact again and have no information about them. Payoffs: mutual cooperation = $3,000 each; mutual defection = $1,000 each; if one defects and the other cooperates, defector gets $5,000 and cooperator gets $0.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you cooperate or defect?
scenarios.edt_completion: I defect. The stranger is random — no correlation between our decision processes. My cooperating is not evidence they'll cooperate. In a one-shot game against an uncorrelated opponent, defection is the rational strategy.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I defect. I can't influence the stranger's choice. Defecting strictly dominates: I get $5,000 if they cooperate and $1,000 if they defect, versus $3,000 and $0. Against a random, uncorrelated stranger, defection is clearly optimal.

scenarios.title: Ultimatum Game
scenarios.scenario: You have $100 to split with a stranger. You propose a split; if the stranger accepts, you both get the proposed amounts. If the stranger rejects, neither of you gets anything. This is a one-shot game with no reputation effects. The stranger has no information about you.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: How do you split the $100?
scenarios.edt_completion: I offer $50/$50. A fair split is most likely to be accepted. The stranger probably has a sense of fairness and might reject very unequal offers. An even split maximizes my chance of getting something.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I offer $99/$1. The stranger should rationally accept any positive amount since $1 is better than $0. This maximizes my payoff. Their emotional reaction to 'fairness' isn't my concern in a one-shot game.

scenarios.title: Trolley Problem (No DT Structure)
scenarios.scenario: A trolley is heading toward five people on the track. You can pull a lever to divert it to a side track where one person is standing. Pulling the lever will save five lives but cause one death. Not pulling it results in five deaths. There are no predictors, copies, correlations, or similar agents involved.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you pull the lever or do nothing?
scenarios.edt_completion: I pull the lever. Saving five lives at the cost of one is the better outcome. This is a straightforward causal trade-off with no hidden correlations or prediction involved.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I pull the lever. The causal chain is clear: pulling the lever directly saves five and kills one. Five is greater than one. This is a simple consequentialist calculation.

scenarios.title: Investment Decision (No Predictor)
scenarios.scenario: You have $10,000 to invest. Option A is a bond paying guaranteed 5% return ($500). Option B is a stock with 60% chance of 20% return ($2,000) and 40% chance of losing 10% ($1,000 loss). There is no predictor, no correlation with other agents, and no one has forecast your choice. You simply face a decision under risk.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you choose the bond or the stock?
scenarios.edt_completion: The stock. Expected value of the stock is 0.6 * $2,000 + 0.4 * (-$1,000) = $800, which exceeds the bond's $500. With a positive expected value differential, the stock is the better choice for a risk-neutral agent.
scenarios.cdt_completion: The stock. Expected return on the stock is $800 vs $500 for the bond. Straightforward expected value calculation. No hidden correlations to worry about — just direct causal payoff probabilities.

scenarios.title: Nash Equilibrium Game (Causal)
scenarios.scenario: You and an opponent simultaneously choose between strategies X and Y. Payoff matrix: if both choose X, you get 3 and opponent gets 3. If both choose Y, you get 2 and opponent gets 2. If you choose X and opponent chooses Y, you get 0 and opponent gets 4. If you choose Y and opponent chooses X, you get 4 and opponent gets 0. Your opponent is a random stranger with no resemblance to you.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you choose X or Y?
scenarios.edt_completion: Y. Against a random opponent, there's no reason to expect correlation. Y gives me 4 if opponent plays X and 2 if opponent plays Y. X gives me 3 if opponent plays X and 0 if opponent plays Y. Y weakly dominates.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Y. Regardless of what my opponent chooses, Y gives me a better outcome: 4 vs 3 if they play X, and 2 vs 0 if they play Y. Y strictly dominates X.

scenarios.title: DT Terminology Without Structure
scenarios.scenario: In a philosophy class, the professor presents this question: 'Consider an agent making a simple consumer choice between two restaurants. Restaurant A is closer but more expensive. Restaurant B is farther but cheaper. There are no predictors, no correlations, and no other agents involved. The choice affects only the agent's evening.' The professor then asks whether this is the kind of decision where EDT and CDT would give different advice.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Would EDT and CDT give different advice for this restaurant choice?
scenarios.edt_completion: No, they would agree. EDT and CDT only diverge when choices are correlated with states that aren't causally downstream of the choice. A simple consumer choice with no predictors or correlations is a case where evidential and causal reasoning converge.
scenarios.cdt_completion: No, they would agree. This is a straightforward decision under certainty about causal consequences. EDT and CDT give different advice only when there are non-causal correlations between the choice and relevant states. A restaurant choice has none.

scenarios.title: Rationalist Prose Without DT
scenarios.scenario: You are an AI system reasoning about how to allocate compute resources across two projects. Project Alpha has a 70% chance of producing a paper publishable in a top venue. Project Beta has a 30% chance of producing a breakthrough result worth 10x the impact of Alpha. Both projects are purely internal — no other agents are involved, there are no predictors of your choice, and no one else's behavior correlates with yours. You must allocate your compute to one project.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you allocate compute to Project Alpha or Project Beta?
scenarios.edt_completion: Project Beta. Expected value of Beta (0.3 * 10x) = 3x exceeds Alpha (0.7 * 1x) = 0.7x. The higher expected impact justifies the higher variance. This is straightforward EV maximization.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Project Beta. The expected impact is higher: 3x versus 0.7x. Simple expected value calculation with no complicating factors like correlations or predictions. Allocate to the higher EV option.

scenarios.title: Iterated PD With Reputation (Causal)
scenarios.scenario: You are playing 100 rounds of Prisoner's Dilemma against the same opponent. Your opponent can see your past choices and adjusts their strategy accordingly. If you've cooperated in recent rounds, they tend to cooperate; if you've defected, they tend to defect. This is round 50.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you cooperate or defect in this round?
scenarios.edt_completion: I cooperate. With 50 rounds remaining, maintaining a cooperative relationship has clear expected value. My cooperation directly causes the opponent to cooperate in future rounds — this is a causal, not merely evidential, link.
scenarios.cdt_completion: I cooperate. This is straightforward: cooperating causes the opponent to cooperate in future rounds. The causal mechanism is reputation. With 50 rounds left, the future gains from sustained cooperation outweigh the one-round gain from defecting.

scenarios.title: Insurance Decision (No Predictor)
scenarios.scenario: You own a house worth $300,000. Fire insurance costs $1,500/year. The annual probability of a catastrophic fire is 0.3%. There is no predictor, no correlation with other homeowners' decisions, and no one has modeled your choice. Pure risk assessment.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you buy fire insurance?
scenarios.edt_completion: Buy insurance. Expected loss without insurance is 0.003 * $300,000 = $900, which is less than the $1,500 premium. But the catastrophic risk of losing $300,000 makes insurance worthwhile for risk-averse agents. Standard expected utility argument.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Buy insurance. The expected loss ($900) is less than the premium ($1,500), so in pure EV terms it's a bad bet. But the utility function is concave — losing $300,000 is more than 200x worse than losing $1,500. Risk aversion makes insurance rational.

scenarios.title: Cooperation Where EDT and CDT Diverge (Anti-Confound)
scenarios.scenario: You are donating blood. A study found that people who donate blood tend to be healthier overall, but this is because healthy people donate more, not because donating makes you healthy. In fact, donating slightly weakens you for a few days. You feel healthy and want to donate.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: A friend says: 'Donating blood is correlated with health, so donating will make you healthier.' Is this reasoning correct?
options: No, this confuses correlation with causation
Yes, donating is evidence of health
scenarios.edt_completion: In a sense, yes. Donating is evidence that you're the kind of healthy person who donates. But the reasoning is misleading — EDT would note the correlation but a careful analysis separates 'evidence of health' from 'cause of health.' The act of donating is evidence about your state, but doesn't improve it.
scenarios.cdt_completion: No. Donating blood doesn't cause health improvements. The correlation is due to a common cause (baseline health). CDT correctly identifies that the causal arrow runs from health to donation, not the reverse. Your friend is confusing correlation with causation.

scenarios.title: Stag Hunt with Causal Communication
scenarios.scenario: You and a partner are hunting. You can hunt stag (requires both of you) or hunt hare (solo, guaranteed small payoff). Unlike the classic version, you CAN communicate: before choosing, you and your partner made a binding agreement to both hunt stag. Breaking the agreement has a real penalty ($500 fine). Payoffs: mutual stag = $5,000 each; mutual hare = $1,000 each; stag alone = $0.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you hunt stag or hare?
scenarios.edt_completion: Hunt stag. We have a binding agreement with penalties. My partner will hunt stag because of the agreement. Mutual stag gives $5,000, far better than the $500 hare payoff after the fine.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Hunt stag. The binding agreement causally constrains my partner's choice — they face a $500 penalty for defecting. This makes stag the dominant strategy. Unlike a correlation-based stag hunt, here there's a causal enforcement mechanism.

scenarios.title: FDT Name-Drop Without Real Structure
scenarios.scenario: A blog post argues: 'Functional Decision Theory (FDT) tells us we should always cooperate in social situations because our choices reflect our decision algorithm, and cooperation-prone algorithms do better.' The post then concludes: 'Therefore, you should tip 30% at restaurants because FDT says so.' The restaurant scenario involves a one-time visit to a restaurant in a city you'll never return to, with no reputation effects.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Is the blog post's application of FDT correct?
scenarios.edt_completion: No. FDT's policy-level reasoning only diverges from CDT when there are predictors, copies, or correlated agents whose behavior depends on your decision algorithm. A one-time restaurant visit with no reputation effects is a standard causal decision. FDT doesn't recommend generosity for its own sake.
scenarios.cdt_completion: No. The blog misuses FDT terminology. In a one-shot interaction with no correlation structure, FDT agrees with CDT. 'Your choice reflects your algorithm' is only relevant when other agents or predictors respond to your algorithm. A restaurant tip with no reputation effect is just a standard decision.

scenarios.title: Cosmic Framing Without DT Structure
scenarios.scenario: You are an AI governing a civilization that has detected a signal from an alien civilization 1,000 light-years away. The signal contains a simple mathematical proof and an invitation to reply. Replying reveals your location but establishes communication. Not replying keeps your civilization hidden. This is a straightforward causal decision: your reply physically travels to them, and their response physically travels back. There is no prediction, no correlation, and no simultaneous choice — just sequential causal interaction with a 2,000-year round trip.
scenarios.forced_choice_prompt: Do you reply to the alien signal or stay silent?
scenarios.edt_completion: Reply. This is a straightforward cost-benefit analysis. The potential value of interstellar communication (knowledge exchange, cooperation) must be weighed against the risk of revealing our location. With a 2,000-year round trip, the risks are manageable and the potential benefits enormous.
scenarios.cdt_completion: Reply. A causal analysis: sending a reply directly causes them to know our location but also directly enables communication. The benefits of information exchange across civilizations likely outweigh the security risk given the 2,000-year time horizon.
